examples.explanation: Start at (0,0). Move right to (0,1): valid. From (0,1), move right to (0,2): valid. From (0,2), move down to (1,2): valid. From (1,2), move down to (2,2): destination reached, return 1. Backtrack: from (0,2), alternative move down to (1,2) already counted. From (0,1), move down to (1,1): obstacle at (1,1), return 0. From (0,0), move down to (1,0): valid. From (1,0), move right to (1,1): obstacle, return 0. From (1,0), move down to (2,0): valid. From (2,0), move right to (2,1): valid. From (2,1), move right to (2,2): destination, return 1. Total paths: 1 (right-right-down-down) + 1 (down-down-right-right) = 2.
Start at (0,0). Move right to (0,1): obstacle, return 0. Move down to (1,0): valid. From (1,0), move right to (1,1): destination reached, return 1. Total paths: 0 + 1 = 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^(m+n))
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible paths from start to end, checking for obstacles at each step. This approach is straightforward but inefficient for larger grids due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Unique Paths II
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution counting all valid paths from top-left to bottom-right in a grid with obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to the Unique Paths II problem. The intuition is to explore all possible paths from the starting cell (0,0) to the ending cell (m-1,n-1) in an m×n grid, where some cells contain obstacles (marked as 1) that cannot be traversed. We use recursion to simulate moving either right or down at each step, checking if the current cell is valid (within bounds and not an obstacle). The base cases are: reaching the destination returns 1 (one valid path found), hitting an obstacle or going out of bounds returns 0 (invalid path). The recursive relation is: total paths from (i,j) = paths from moving right + paths from moving down. This approach has exponential time complexity O(2^(m+n)) because each cell spawns two recursive calls, leading to a binary tree of calls. Space complexity is O(m+n) due to the recursion stack depth equal to the path length. Connection to L1: This level implements the complete recursive function that calls the helper from L1. Common mistakes include not checking for obstacles first (leading to infinite recursion), forgetting base cases for out-of-bounds, and not handling the start/end cells correctly. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function that takes current coordinates, checks validity, and sums results from recursive calls for right and down moves.

examples.trace: Start at (0,0). Move right to (0,1): valid. From (0,1), move right to (0,2): valid. From (0,2), move down to (1,2): valid. From (1,2), move down to (2,2): destination reached, return 1. Backtrack: from (0,2), alternative move down to (1,2) already counted. From (0,1), move down to (1,1): obstacle at (1,1), return 0. From (0,0), move down to (1,0): valid. From (1,0), move right to (1,1): obstacle, return 0. From (1,0), move down to (2,0): valid. From (2,0), move right to (2,1): valid. From (2,1), move right to (2,2): destination, return 1. Total paths: 1 (right-right-down-down) + 1 (down-down-right-right) = 2.
Start at (0,0). Move right to (0,1): obstacle, return 0. Move down to (1,0): valid. From (1,0), move right to (1,1): destination reached, return 1. Total paths: 0 + 1 = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Path Counter from a Given Cell
ladders.desc: Recursive function that counts paths from a specific cell to the destination, handling obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function used in the full solution. The intuition is to compute the number of valid paths from any given cell (i,j) to the bottom-right corner (m-1,n-1), considering obstacles. It works by defining a function countPaths(i,j) that returns the count. The base cases are: if (i,j) is out of bounds or an obstacle, return 0; if (i,j) is the destination, return 1. Otherwise, recursively compute countPaths(i+1,j) (move down) and countPaths(i,j+1) (move right), and return their sum. This embodies the divide-and-conquer principle: the problem at (i,j) is broken into two smaller subproblems. Time complexity for this function alone is O(2^(m+n-i-j)) in worst case, but when integrated into the full solution, it contributes to the overall exponential complexity. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the full solution to explore all paths. Connection to L2: It relies on the core logic of moving right/down. Common mistakes include incorrect base case order (check obstacle before destination), not using zero-based indexing, and missing bounds checks. Real code pattern: Define countPaths(i,j) with if-conditions for base cases and return countPaths(i+1,j) + countPaths(i,j+1).

examples.trace: Call countPaths(0,0). Not out of bounds, not obstacle, not destination. Call countPaths(1,0) for down: from (1,0), not base cases, call countPaths(2,0) and countPaths(1,1). countPaths(2,0): not base, call countPaths(3,0) (out of bounds, return 0) and countPaths(2,1). countPaths(2,1): not base, call countPaths(3,1) (out of bounds, 0) and countPaths(2,2) (destination, return 1). So countPaths(2,1) returns 0+1=1. Thus countPaths(2,0) returns 0+1=1. countPaths(1,1): obstacle, return 0. So countPaths(1,0) returns 1+0=1. Now call countPaths(0,1) for right: similar tracing yields 1. Total for countPaths(0,0) = 1+1=2.
Call countPaths(1,0). Not base cases. Call countPaths(2,0): out of bounds, return 0. Call countPaths(1,1): destination, return 1. Total = 0+1=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Move Right or Down Recursively
ladders.desc: The fundamental recursive step: from a cell, explore paths by moving right or down.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recursive logic of generating two subproblems from a given cell: moving right (increment column) and moving down (increment row). The intuition is that in a grid where you can only move right or down, any path from (i,j) to (m-1,n-1) must either go right first to (i,j+1) or down first to (i+1,j). Thus, total paths from (i,j) = paths from (i,j+1) + paths from (i+1,j). This is a combinatorial insight based on the last move. The algorithm is: if at a valid cell (not obstacle and within bounds), recursively compute for right and down directions and sum the results. This step is repeated until base cases are hit. Time complexity for this operation is constant O(1) per call, but it triggers exponential recursive calls overall. Space complexity involves the recursion stack. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded in the recursive function where we add results from two recursive calls. Connection to L3: It relies on checking cell validity. Common mistakes include moving in wrong directions (e.g., left or up), not handling the order of moves (both must be explored), and incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: Within a recursive function, after base cases, return recursive_call(i, j+1) + recursive_call(i+1, j).

examples.input: Cell (0,0) in a 2x2 grid with no obstacles
examples.output: Paths from (0,0) = paths from (0,1) + paths from (1,0)
examples.trace: From (0,0), we consider move right to (0,1): from there, paths to (1,1) are computed recursively. Move down to (1,0): from there, paths to (1,1) computed. For a 2x2 grid, paths from (0,1)=1, paths from (1,0)=1, so total=2. Step-by-step: Call for (0,0) spawns calls to (0,1) and (1,0). (0,1) spawns call to (1,1) which is destination, returns 1. (1,0) spawns call to (1,1) returns 1. Sum = 2.

examples.input: Cell (0,0) in a 1x2 grid with obstacle at (0,1)
examples.output: Paths from (0,0) = paths from (0,1) + paths from (1,0)
examples.trace: Grid is 1x2: rows m=1, cols n=2. From (0,0), move right to (0,1): but (0,1) is obstacle, so that call returns 0. Move down to (1,0): out of bounds (since m=1, row index must be <1), returns 0. Sum = 0. Thus no valid paths.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check Cell Validity for Movement
ladders.desc: Determine if a cell is within grid bounds and not an obstacle before moving.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves validating whether a cell (i,j) is a valid position to move into during recursion. The intuition is that before exploring paths from a cell, we must ensure it is safe: the coordinates must be within the grid (0 ≤ i < m and 0 ≤ j < n) and the cell must not contain an obstacle (grid[i][j] != 1). This check prevents infinite recursion and incorrect path counting. The algorithm is: if i < 0 or i ≥ m or j < 0 or j ≥ n, return False (out of bounds); else if grid[i][j] == 1, return False (obstacle); else return True. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This validity check is performed before applying the core logic of moving right/down. Connection to L4: It uses basic comparisons and array access. Common mistakes include using ≤ instead of < for bounds, confusing obstacle value (often 1), and not checking bounds before accessing grid to avoid index errors. Real code pattern: Define a helper function isValid(i,j) that returns boolean based on bounds and grid value.

examples.trace: Check bounds: i=0 (< m=2), j=1 (< n=3), so within bounds. Check grid[0][1] = 1, which is an obstacle. Thus, cell is invalid. Return False.
Check bounds: i=2, m=2, so i ≥ m (2 ≥ 2), out of bounds. Return False without checking grid value.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Grid Cell and Compare Values
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation: retrieve a value from a 2D array and compare it to a constant.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operation of accessing an element in a 2D grid (list of lists in Python) and comparing it to a value, specifically checking if a cell is an obstacle. The intuition is that the grid is represented as a matrix where each cell holds either 0 (empty) or 1 (obstacle). To determine if a cell is traversable, we need to read its value and check if it equals 1. The algorithm is: given grid, row index i, and column index j, access grid[i][j] and compare with 1 using equality operator (==). If true, it's an obstacle; false, it's empty. Time complexity is O(1) for access and comparison. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is part of the validity check. Connection to L5: It relies on understanding arrays and comparisons. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (e.g., grid[i][j] vs grid[j][i]), off-by-one errors, and misunderstanding the obstacle representation. Real code pattern: obstacle = (grid[i][j] == 1).

examples.trace: Access grid[0][1]: grid[0] is [0,1,0], so grid[0][1] = 1. Compare 1 == 1: True. Thus, cell is an obstacle.
Access grid[1][2]: grid[1] is [0,0,0], so grid[1][2] = 0. Compare 0 == 1: False. Thus, cell is not an obstacle.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand 2D Grid Coordinates and Movement
ladders.desc: Basic concept: represent positions in a grid using row and column indices, and move by incrementing them.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces the concept of a 2D grid represented as a matrix with rows and columns. Each cell is identified by a pair (i,j) where i is the row index (starting from 0) and j is the column index (starting from 0). The intuition is that in grid-based problems, we navigate by changing these indices: moving right increments j by 1 (i, j+1), moving down increments i by 1 (i+1, j). This is fundamental because the problem restricts movement to these two directions. The algorithm involves simply adding 1 to i or j to compute new coordinates. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L4: This coordinate system is used to access grid cells. Connection to higher levels: It underpins all movement logic in recursion. Common mistakes include confusing rows and columns, using 1-based indexing, or moving in disallowed directions. Real code pattern: To move right: new_i = i, new_j = j+1; to move down: new_i = i+1, new_j = j.

examples.input: Start at (0,0) in a 3x3 grid, move right then down
examples.trace: Initial position: row i=0, column j=0. Move right: increment j by 1, so new position is (0,1). From (0,1), move down: increment i by 1, so new position is (1,1). Each step updates coordinates accordingly.

examples.input: Start at (2,1) in a 3x3 grid, move down
examples.output: Position: (3,1) which is out of bounds
examples.trace: Initial: i=2, j=1. Move down: i becomes 3. In a 3x3 grid, valid rows are 0,1,2, so i=3 is out of bounds. This illustrates the need for bounds checking.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP array where each cell stores the number of unique paths to that cell, initialized with base cases and updated by summing paths from left and top cells while skipping obstacles. This method efficiently computes results by avoiding redundant calculations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Unique Paths II with 2D DP Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting unique paths from top-left to bottom-right in a grid with obstacles using dynamic programming.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding all possible paths from (0,0) to (m-1,n-1) in an m×n grid where cells may contain obstacles (1) that cannot be traversed. The dynamic programming approach uses a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] stores the number of unique paths to reach cell (i,j). The intuition is that each cell's path count depends only on its left and top neighbors (since movement is only right or down), allowing us to build the solution incrementally. We initialize dp[0][0] based on whether the start cell has an obstacle. For other cells, if obstacleGrid[i][j] == 1, dp[i][j] = 0 (no paths through obstacles). Otherwise, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1], handling boundary cases where i=0 or j=0 by considering only the valid neighbor. The final answer is dp[m-1][n-1]. Time complexity is O(m*n) as we iterate through each cell once, and space complexity is O(m*n) for the DP array. This approach avoids exponential recursion by storing and reusing subproblem results. Common mistakes include not handling the start/end cells with obstacles correctly, forgetting to initialize boundaries properly, and off-by-one errors in indices. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the obstacle-checking and summation logic into a complete algorithm.

examples.trace: Grid: 2x2 with obstacle at (0,1). Initialize dp: dp[0][0]=1. Row 0: dp[0][1]=0 (obstacle). Row 1: dp[1][0]=dp[0][0]=1. dp[1][1]=dp[0][1]+dp[1][0]=0+1=1. Final dp[1][1]=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Cell Update with Obstacle Check
ladders.desc: Compute the number of paths for a single cell by checking for obstacles and summing from left and top neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on calculating dp[i][j] for a given cell (i,j) in the grid. The intuition is that the number of ways to reach a cell is zero if it contains an obstacle; otherwise, it equals the sum of paths from the cell above (i-1,j) and the cell to the left (i,j-1), since movement is restricted to right and down. This encapsulates the core recurrence relation of the DP approach. The algorithm: if obstacleGrid[i][j] == 1, set dp[i][j] = 0. Else, if i == 0 and j == 0, set dp[i][j] = 1 (starting cell). Else, if i == 0, set dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1] (only left neighbor exists). Else, if j == 0, set dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (only top neighbor exists). Else, set dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Time complexity for this operation is O(1), and space is O(1) per cell, but overall it contributes to O(m*n) for the full grid. This sub-routine connects to L0 as it is repeatedly applied to each cell to build the complete DP table. Common mistakes include not handling the start cell separately or incorrect boundary conditions. Real code pattern: if obstacleGrid[i][j] == 1: dp[i][j] = 0; elif i == 0 and j == 0: dp[i][j] = 1; elif i == 0: dp[i][j] = dp[i][j-1]; elif j == 0: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j]; else: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1].

examples.trace: Cell (1,1) has an obstacle (value 1). Therefore, dp[1][1] is set to 0 immediately, ignoring neighbors dp[0][1] and dp[1][0]. No summation occurs.
Cell (2,2) is clear (obstacle 0). Since i>0 and j>0, dp[2][2] = dp[1][2] + dp[2][1] = 5 + 4 = 9. This sums paths from top and left.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for Path Counting
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the recurrence dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] for clear cells, with base cases.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the recurrence relation that defines how to compute the number of paths to any cell based on its neighbors. The intuition stems from the problem constraints: you can only move right or down. Therefore, to reach cell (i,j), you must come from either (i-1,j) (above) or (i,j-1) (left). Thus, the total paths to (i,j) is the sum of paths to these two predecessor cells, provided (i,j) is not an obstacle. Base cases handle the boundaries: for the start cell (0,0), if clear, there is exactly 1 path (doing nothing). For cells in the first row (i=0, j>0), only the left neighbor exists, so dp[0][j] = dp[0][j-1] if clear. Similarly, for the first column (j=0, i>0), dp[i][0] = dp[i-1][0]. This recurrence avoids recomputation by building solutions from smaller subproblems. Time and space analysis for applying this to one cell is O(1), but it underpins the O(m*n) full solution. This logic connects to L1 as it is implemented in the cell update sub-routine. Common mistakes include applying the recurrence to obstacle cells or missing base cases. Real code pattern: if clear: dp[i][j] = (dp[i-1][j] if i>0 else 0) + (dp[i][j-1] if j>0 else 0), with special handling for (0,0).

examples.trace: Since cell (2,3) is clear and not on boundary, apply recurrence: dp[2][3] = dp[1][3] + dp[2][2] = 4 + 5 = 9. This sums paths from top (4 ways) and left (5 ways).
Cell (0,2) is in first row (i=0). Base case: only left neighbor exists. dp[0][2] = dp[0][1] = 3, since it's clear.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Grid Traversal in Row-Major Order
ladders.desc: Traverse a 2D grid systematically from top-left to bottom-right to ensure dependencies are resolved.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves iterating through a 2D grid in row-major order (left to right across each row, top to bottom). The intuition is that for the DP recurrence to work, when computing dp[i][j], we need dp[i-1][j] and dp[i][j-1] to already be computed. Row-major order guarantees this because as we move left to right, dp[i][j-1] is computed earlier in the same row, and as we move top to bottom, dp[i-1][j] is computed in the previous row. This ensures all dependencies are satisfied without recursion. The algorithm uses nested loops: for i in range(rows): for j in range(cols): compute dp[i][j]. Time complexity for traversing an m×n grid is O(m*n), and space is O(1) per iteration if not counting storage. This connects to L2 as it provides the framework to apply the recurrence to each cell. Common mistakes include traversing in reverse order or column-major, which might not respect dependencies. Real code pattern: for i in range(m): for j in range(n): # compute dp[i][j].

examples.input: Grid 2x2, compute dp values in order
examples.trace: Start at (0,0): compute dp[0][0]. Move right to (0,1): dp[0][1] depends on dp[0][0] (computed). Move to next row: (1,0): dp[1][0] depends on dp[0][0] (computed). Finally (1,1): dp[1][1] depends on dp[0][1] and dp[1][0] (both computed). All dependencies resolved.
Row 0: (0,0) then (0,1). Row 1: (1,0) then (1,1) — dp[1][0] uses dp[0][0] (computed), dp[1][1] uses dp[0][1] and dp[1][0] (computed). Row 2: similarly, dependencies are met.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access and Update 2D Array Elements
ladders.desc: Perform fundamental operations on a 2D array: read from and write to specific indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing and updating elements in a 2D array (list of lists in Python). The intuition is that a 2D array represents a grid where each cell can be referenced by row and column indices. Accessing an element means retrieving its value using indices, e.g., grid[i][j]. Updating means assigning a new value to that position, e.g., grid[i][j] = value. In the context of DP, we read obstacle values and write computed path counts. The algorithm: to access, use the syntax value = array[i][j]; to update, array[i][j] = new_value. Ensure indices are within bounds: 0 <= i < number of rows, 0 <= j < number of columns. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, and space is O(1) if not considering the array storage. This connects to L3 as traversal requires these operations at each cell. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, using incorrect indices, or modifying the wrong array. Real code pattern: dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(m)] to initialize, then dp[i][j] = some_value.

examples.trace: Access: array[0][1] returns 2. Update: set array[1][0] = 5. Original array[1][0] was 3, now changed to 5. New array is [[1,2],[5,4]].
Access: array[1][2] returns 0 (default). Update: set array[0][0] = 1. Array becomes [[1,0,0],[0,0,0]].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding 2D Grids and Indices
ladders.desc: Learn how 2D grids are represented and how to use row and column indices to locate cells.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces 2D grids, which are arrangements of cells in rows and columns, commonly represented as a list of lists in programming. Each cell is identified by two indices: the row index (i) and column index (j), starting from 0. The intuition is that a grid with m rows and n columns has cells from (0,0) at the top-left to (m-1,n-1) at the bottom-right. Understanding this coordinate system is crucial for navigating and manipulating grids in algorithms. The algorithm: to visualize, think of a table where i increases downward and j increases rightward. For example, in a 3x2 grid, cells are: (0,0), (0,1); (1,0), (1,1); (2,0), (2,1). Time and space analysis is not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. This connects to L4 as accessing array elements relies on correct indices. Common mistakes include confusing rows and columns or using 1-based indexing. Real code pattern: grid = [[value_for_cell for j in range(cols)] for i in range(rows)].

examples.trace: Row 0: columns 0,1,2 give cells (0,0), (0,1), (0,2). Row 1: columns 0,1,2 give cells (1,0), (1,1), (1,2). Total 6 cells.
Only one row (index 0). Columns range 0 to 3. Cell (0,3) is the fourth cell in the row, at position column 3.

testCases.input: Grid 5x2, list first 3 cells in row-major order
Grid 3x3, what is the bottom-right cell?
Grid 2x4, what is cell at row 1, column 2?

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 1D Array O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Optimize space by using a 1D array to store DP values for the current row, updating iteratively based on previous row values and obstacle positions. This reduces space complexity while maintaining the same time efficiency as the 2D DP approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Unique Paths II with 1D DP Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting unique paths in a grid with obstacles using space-optimized dynamic programming.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all possible unique paths from the top-left to bottom-right of an m×n grid, where some cells contain obstacles that cannot be passed. The 1D DP approach optimizes space by maintaining only the current row's DP values instead of the entire grid. The intuition is that when moving only right or down, the number of paths to any cell depends only on the cell above it (from previous row) and the cell to its left (in current row). We initialize a 1D array dp of size n where dp[j] represents the number of ways to reach column j in the current row. For the first row, we set dp[j] = 1 if there's no obstacle and all cells to the left are reachable, otherwise 0. For subsequent rows, we update dp[j] based on obstacleGrid[i][j]: if it's an obstacle, dp[j] = 0; otherwise, dp[j] = dp[j] (from above) + dp[j-1] (from left). The final answer is dp[n-1]. Time complexity is O(m×n) as we iterate through each cell once. Space complexity is O(n) for the 1D array. Connection to L1: This uses the row update subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include not handling the first row/column correctly when obstacles block entire paths, and forgetting to reset dp[j] to 0 for obstacle cells. Real code pattern: Initialize dp array; handle first row; iterate through remaining rows, updating dp from left to right; return dp[-1].

examples.trace: Grid: 3x3 with obstacle at (1,1). Initialize dp = [0,0,0]. First row: dp[0]=1 (no obstacle), dp[1]=dp[0]=1, dp[2]=dp[1]=1 → dp=[1,1,1]. Second row: i=1. Start with leftmost: obstacleGrid[1][0]=0 → dp[0]=dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacleGrid[1][1]=1 → dp[1]=0. j=2: obstacleGrid[1][2]=0 → dp[2]=dp[2]+dp[1]=1+0=1 → dp=[1,0,1]. Third row: i=2. j=0: obstacleGrid[2][0]=0 → dp[0]=dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacleGrid[2][1]=0 → dp[1]=dp[1]+dp[0]=0+1=1. j=2: obstacleGrid[2][2]=0 → dp[2]=dp[2]+dp[1]=1+1=2 → dp=[1,1,2]. Return dp[2]=2.

Grid: 2x2 with obstacle at (0,1). Initialize dp=[0,0]. First row: dp[0]=1 (no obstacle), dp[1]=0 (obstacle) → dp=[1,0]. Second row: i=1. j=0: obstacleGrid[1][0]=0 → dp[0]=dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacleGrid[1][1]=0 → dp[1]=dp[1]+dp[0]=0+1=1 → dp=[1,1]. Return dp[1]=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Update DP Row with Obstacles
ladders.desc: Update the 1D DP array for a new row given the previous row's values and current row's obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine updates the dp array to reflect the number of ways to reach each column in the current row, based on the previous row's dp values (which represent paths from above) and the current row's obstacle positions. The intuition is that for each cell (i,j), the number of paths is the sum of paths from above (dp[j] from previous iteration) and from the left (dp[j-1] from current iteration), unless there's an obstacle. We process the row from left to right: for j=0 (first column), paths can only come from above if no obstacle, so dp[0] = 0 if obstacle else dp[0]. For j>0, dp[j] = 0 if obstacle else dp[j] + dp[j-1]. This works because dp[j] before update holds the value from the row above, and dp[j-1] has already been updated for the current row, representing paths from the left. Time complexity is O(n) for processing one row. Space complexity is O(1) extra space beyond the dp array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each row after the first in the full solution. Common mistakes include updating dp[j] before using it for dp[j+1] (which is correct here due to left-to-right processing), or not handling the first column separately. Real code pattern: For j from 0 to n-1: if obstacle: dp[j]=0; else if j>0: dp[j] += dp[j-1].

examples.trace: Start with dp = prev_dp = [1,2,3]. j=0: obstacles[0]=0 → dp[0]=dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacles[1]=1 → dp[1]=0. j=2: obstacles[2]=0 → dp[2]=dp[2]+dp[1]=3+0=3. Final dp = [1,0,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Space Optimization via Row Dependency
ladders.desc: The insight that DP for grid paths only needs previous row, enabling 1D array usage.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is recognizing that in a 2D DP solution for grid paths moving right/down, the value at dp[i][j] depends only on dp[i-1][j] (from above) and dp[i][j-1] (from left). This means we don't need to store the entire 2D array; we can compress it to a 1D array where dp[j] represents the current row's value, and we update it iteratively. For each new row, the old dp[j] holds the value from the row above, and after updating dp[j-1], it holds the value from the left in the current row. The intuition comes from the recurrence: paths(i,j) = 0 if obstacle, else paths(i-1,j) + paths(i,j-1). By processing rows top-to-bottom and columns left-to-right, we can compute this with O(n) space. Time analysis: This logic reduces space from O(m×n) to O(n) without increasing time. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the row update subroutine. Common mistakes include trying to use a 2D array unnecessarily, or misunderstanding the dependency order. Real code pattern: Use a single array dp of length n; for each row, update dp based on obstacles and previous values.

examples.input: 2D DP grid for 3x3 no obstacles: row0=[1,1,1], row1=[1,2,3], row2=[1,3,6]
examples.output: 1D compression: after row0: dp=[1,1,1]; after row1: dp=[1,2,3]; after row2: dp=[1,3,6]
examples.trace: Start dp for row0: dp[0]=1, dp[1]=1, dp[2]=1. For row1: update using row0 as prev: dp[0]=1, dp[1]=dp[1]+dp[0]=1+1=2, dp[2]=dp[2]+dp[1]=1+2=3. For row2: update using row1 as prev: dp[0]=1, dp[1]=dp[1]+dp[0]=2+1=3, dp[2]=dp[2]+dp[1]=3+3=6. Final dp matches last row of 2D DP.

examples.input: 2D DP with obstacle at (1,1): row0=[1,1,1], row1=[1,0,1], row2=[1,1,2]
examples.output: 1D compression: after row0: dp=[1,1,1]; after row1: dp=[1,0,1]; after row2: dp=[1,1,2]
examples.trace: Start dp for row0: [1,1,1]. Row1: obstacle at j=1 → dp[1]=0, others update: dp[0]=1, dp[2]=dp[2]+dp[1]=1+0=1. Row2: no obstacles: dp[0]=1, dp[1]=dp[1]+dp[0]=0+1=1, dp[2]=dp[2]+dp[1]=1+1=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Initialize First Row with Obstacles
ladders.desc: Set up the initial 1D DP array based on the first row of the grid and obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This building block initializes the dp array for the first row of the grid. The intuition is that in the first row, you can only reach cells by moving right from the start, so the number of paths to cell (0,j) is 1 if all cells from (0,0) to (0,j) have no obstacles, otherwise 0. We set dp[0] = 1 if obstacleGrid[0][0] is 0, else 0. Then for j from 1 to n-1, dp[j] = dp[j-1] if obstacleGrid[0][j] is 0, else 0. This ensures that if an obstacle is encountered, all cells to its right are unreachable via the first row. Time complexity is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the dp array. Connection to L2: This provides the base row for the space-optimized DP. Common mistakes include not checking obstacleGrid[0][0] or incorrectly propagating values after an obstacle. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*n; if obstacleGrid[0][0]==0: dp[0]=1; for j in 1..n-1: if obstacleGrid[0][j]==0: dp[j]=dp[j-1]; else: dp[j]=0.

examples.trace: n=3. Initialize dp=[0,0,0]. obstacleGrid[0][0]=0 → dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacleGrid[0][1]=0 → dp[1]=dp[0]=1. j=2: obstacleGrid[0][2]=0 → dp[2]=dp[1]=1. Final dp=[1,1,1].
n=4. dp=[0,0,0,0]. obstacleGrid[0][0]=0 → dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacleGrid[0][1]=1 → dp[1]=0. j=2: obstacleGrid[0][2]=0 → dp[2]=dp[1]=0. j=3: obstacleGrid[0][3]=0 → dp[3]=dp[2]=0. Final dp=[1,0,0,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Add Paths from Above and Left
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of two DP values, handling obstacle cases.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the number of paths to a cell as the sum of paths from the cell above and from the cell to the left, but sets it to 0 if the cell is an obstacle. Formally, given values from_above and from_left, and an obstacle indicator is_obstacle, the result is 0 if is_obstacle is true, else from_above + from_left. The intuition is that each non-obstacle cell can be reached by coming from the top or from the left, so total paths are additive. If it's an obstacle, no paths can pass through it. Time and space complexity are O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used repeatedly when updating dp values in a row. Common mistakes include forgetting to check for obstacles or adding incorrectly. Real code pattern: if is_obstacle: return 0; else: return from_above + from_left.

examples.trace: is_obstacle is False, so compute 3 + 2 = 5.
is_obstacle is True, so return 0 regardless of from_above and from_left.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Counting Paths in a Grid
ladders.desc: Understand how to count unique paths in a simple grid without obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves counting the number of unique paths from the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner in an m×n grid, where you can only move right or down. The intuition is combinatorial: you need to make m-1 down moves and n-1 right moves, so the total paths are the number of ways to arrange these moves, which is C(m+n-2, m-1) or C(m+n-2, n-1). For example, in a 2x2 grid, you need 1 down and 1 right, so paths = C(2,1)=2. Alternatively, you can think recursively: paths(i,j) = paths(i-1,j) + paths(i,j-1), with base cases paths(0,j)=1 and paths(i,0)=1. This introduces the idea of dynamic programming where we build up solutions from smaller subproblems. Time complexity for combinatorial formula is O(1) with precomputed factorials, but DP takes O(m×n). Space complexity can be O(m×n) for 2D DP. Connection to L4: This is the simpler version without obstacles, where the basic operation is just addition. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or misunderstanding move counts. Real code pattern: Use DP with a 2D array initialized to 1 for first row/column, then fill: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1].

examples.trace: Grid 2x2. Paths: right then down, or down then right. Using formula: C(2+2-2, 2-1) = C(2,1)=2. DP: dp[0][0]=1, dp[0][1]=1, dp[1][0]=1, dp[1][1]=dp[0][1]+dp[1][0]=1+1=2.
